01 Introduction & Overview - YouTube.mp4
How does your company create value? It seems like a simple question, but in my experience, most entrepreneurs have no idea how to answer it. So I'm going to ask you again: what is your value creation process? Do you know where value actually rests in your business? Now, if your answer is no, or if you're just a little unsure, then I have good news. By the end of this session, you will have an answer to this question because our goal for this module is to visually document how your company creates value through a process that we call value engine mapping. Value engines are the first tool that make up the broader, scalable operating system framework, and they're also the tool that forms the foundation of the set of algorithms that we'll be documenting over the next few sessions.
Now, let me give you the backstory for how we discovered value engines. And I want you to tell me if any of this sounds familiar, if you've ever kind of been through one of these situations yourself. Now, for as long as I've been in business, I've... heard that you need to document your company's systems and processes. Have you heard that right? You need to have checklists. You need to have standard operating procedures for everything you do, right? That's what we've all been told. And we did that. I mean, I remember sending out a massive mandate across the entire company declaring that everyone needs to write down and document in clear, step-by-step detail their five most common and critical tasks. We spent an entire month on this project and we nearly shut down the company in the process.
I mean, it was a really, really big deal. And when we were finished, we had a beautiful binder filled with dozens of checklists that, wait for it, literally no one ever used. And that's not an exaggeration, by the way. None of these checklists that we spent so much time creating were ever used on a day-to-day basis. It was one of the single biggest wastes of time in the company's history. And I remember being so frustrated because I knew that our people cared. I mean, heck, one of our core values is 'document and share what you know.' So I knew the team cared and I knew they wanted to do good work. And yet. The checklists just weren't getting used. Why? Right? Why? Why did no one ever use them when we put so much...
work into their creation, and when everyone agreed that this project was super important. Well, I had to find out, so I assembled a company-wide meeting to get to the bottom of it. When I polled the team, the answers ranged from, you know what, Ryan, it's just that we already know how to do this stuff, or it just takes too long, or just down to the, hey, I forgot, right? Now, these were all semi-helpful, but none of them really felt like the real answer. So. In a moment of exhausted frustration, I remember saying, 'Okay, you know what? Screw it.' How do products get made? All right. We're a company. We make products. So how does that happen? And then, you know, when, you know, someone buys one of our products, you know, what happens then?
And while we're at it, how do we get new customers in the first place? Right. We're a company. We got customers. How does that happen? What's the first step in the process? And I remember starting from there. I was like, 'Okay, customers, how do they happen?' And I was standing in our conference room in front of our whiteboard and I had this like stack of sticky notes in my hand. And when someone said something, I just wrote it down on a sticky note and I stuck it to the board. Okay, everyone. So that's the first step in this process. I would ask, does everyone agree? Now, invariably, someone would speak up and say no. You know what? Actually, I think it's this other step that comes first.
So I'd write that one down and I would you know stick that one to the board in front of the other one. Okay, great, I would say. Then what happens? Someone would speak up. I'd write down what they said, and I would stick it to the right of the previous sticky note. And this process continued. Then what happens? Then what happens? Occasionally, someone would speak up and say, well, it depends. If they do X, then this happens. And if they do Y, then this other thing happens. Okay. I said and I turned the square sticky note on an angle to form a diamond and added lines from each point, denoting that there was a simple fork in the road. Okay, let's go down this road for now.
I said, you know, assuming someone makes it to this point, then what happens? Then what? Then what? And if they go down this other path because they didn't show up for their appointment or they ask, you know, for the blue widget instead of the red one. My question was the same. Then what happens? Right? Then what? Then what? Eventually, we reached a point where everyone agreed, 'That's it,' right? This phase of the customer acquisition, or the product fulfillment, or the product creation process is done. When we were finished, I just remember looking at the whiteboard and thinking, 'This is it. This is it.' This is how we as a company create value. Now, there wasn't much to it. It was just a dozen or so sticky notes all connected with...
you know, dry erase marker lines, but it was oddly beautiful in its simplicity. It wasn't a single isolated checklist detailing how to perform a single isolated task. What we had for the first time ever was a visual representation, a map. It shows how we as a company create value by either acquiring a customer or fulfilling on the promises made to that customer. Because let's face it, at the end of the day, that's kind of all businesses really do, isn't it? We make stuff to sell. We sell the stuff we make and we deliver the stuff we make to the customer after we've sold it. That's it. This simple and admittedly ugly mishmash of sticky notes accomplished something in a matter of minutes that we had failed as a company to accomplish in a month.
We finally understood how our company acquired customers and served those customers. And we finally understood how all those disparate tasks sitting in that stupid freaking binder fit together. In other words, as opposed to thinking about our business as a series of just disconnected checklists. We instead saw it as an orchestrated flow where one activity flowed seamlessly to the next, like a beautifully coordinated ballet, a single visual map of our value creation process. Let me tell you. This changed everything. Now that we could see the big picture and understand how value flows throughout the organization, we could look down on it from a 30,000-foot view and ask: 'Which tasks and activities, represented by all these little sticky notes, which ones are truly critical? Which ones actually move the needle?
Which ones do we really need to get right?' When we asked this question, we discovered that really only two or three tasks within a given value engine were truly critical. Sure, the other steps needed to happen, but there were always a handful that we absolutely positively had to get right or the entire value engine would fail. These critical tasks power stages and we ultimately decided to focus all our documentation efforts on only those handful of stages that we truly had to get right. And as for the rest of the checklist, we created, you know the ones that were still sitting in the binder? Well, they wound up getting filed in the circular filing cabinet. Which is code, in case you don't already know, for the trash can.
Now, just to clarify, I'm not saying that checklists and SOPs aren't important. Please don't hear that because they absolutely are. In fact, we're going to talk all about building your business playbook library, which is really just a collection of step-by-step checklists in a later module. But in my experience, most companies in their attempt to document everything wind up documenting nothing. Or worse, they document everything and then use nothing, just like us. The big mistake that everyone is making is they're getting the process of process documentation out of order. Which I acknowledge, by the way, is more than a little bit ironic. So let me be clear. All right. Step one is not to document all your company's critical tasks and activities. Step one is to map your company's core value creation flows and then, and only then, can you identify your power stages— those truly critical actions and activities that need to be documented and measured. But that second step of documenting your power stages, that one's for another day. Today, we're going to start with documenting your company's value engines because that's the real first step. So. Let's get into it.
